title: Lasher wins NY-12 Democratic primary after AI super PACs spend $20M+ on the wrong candidate
subtitle: Micah Lasher took the Manhattan congressional seat with 39% of the vote, beating state assemblyman Alex Bores — the candidate that Anthropic-aligned and OpenAI-aligned super PACs spent eight figures fighting over.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: State assemblymember Micah Lasher won the June 24 Democratic primary in New York's 12th Congressional District — the seat being vacated by retiring Rep. Jerry Nadler — with 39.04% of the vote. Alex Bores finished second at 35.01%; Kennedy scion Jack Schlossberg and former Republican lawyer George Conway trailed behind. The race drew more than $20 million in outside super-PAC spending tied to the AI industry: Leading the Future, a pro-deregulation group connected to OpenAI president Greg Brockman and Andreessen Horowitz, spent roughly $8 million attacking Bores; Jobs and Democracy PAC, backed by Anthropic, spent close to $7 million defending him. Neither side spent meaningfully on Lasher.

What matters here is what didn't happen. Both AI factions chose Bores as the proxy — pro-regulation forces saw him as their guy, pro-innovation forces saw him as the threat to defeat — and they fought a $15M+ shadow campaign over a candidate who came second. The actual winner ran on Bloomberg's endorsement, Hochul's endorsement, and Nadler's endorsement, and didn't need either AI super PAC. Lasher has pledged to pursue the same AI regulation agenda Bores was campaigning on, which means the pro-safety position effectively won — but not through the channel the money was flowing through.

This is now part of a pattern. AI-related super PACs have committed more than $50 million to 2026 elections — roughly $22M from pro-innovation groups, close to $28M from pro-safety groups — and so far the spend hasn't moved races the way the donors wanted. The Fortune analysis on June 26 noted that across 35 races touched by these PACs, the return on the spending is hard to find. Frontier labs are discovering that flooding a primary with attack ads against a state legislator is a different game than lobbying a House committee, and Manhattan voters appear to have read the saturation negative coverage as a reason to vote for someone else.

A takeaway for learners: AI policy will be made by people who weren't picked by AI companies. If you're studying AI governance, watch the candidates the labs ignored — they are the ones who arrive in Washington without an IOU to either faction. Lasher will likely chair a subcommittee or two next term; how he frames AI rules, having been on the receiving end of neither faction's money, is a useful baseline for what 'independent' AI policy actually looks like.

sources.title: Anthropic and OpenAI waged a $27 million proxy war in a Manhattan congressional race. The winner told them both to get lost. — Fortune
New York Democrat Micah Lasher wins House primary that drew big spending from AI groups — NBC News
AI PACs pour $20 million into New York Democratic primary in AI regulation battle — CNBC
Micah Lasher wins crowded Democratic primary for Manhattan U.S. House seat — Gothamist
